description: ClawHQ backlog — derived from PRODUCT.md, ARCHITECTURE.md, README.md (2026-03-18)

items.title: clawhq deploy update — push config and version updates to cloud-deployed agents
items.intent: A sovereign cloud agent that cannot be updated is a liability, not an asset. clawhq deploy update must let users push config changes, version upgrades, and new skills to any cloud-deployed agent without reprovisioning. The agent grows pillar must hold for cloud deployments.
items.description: After clawhq deploy provisions a cloud agent, users need to push updates without reprovisioning from scratch. Three update scenarios: (1) config change — update blueprint vars or credentials without restart, (2) clawhq version upgrade — pull latest, rebuild Docker image, restart agent, (3) skill install/remove — evolve the cloud agent remotely. NOTE: Implementation is ~90% complete — update.ts has full SSH execution engine with 3 modes, shell escaping, timeouts, abort signals. ssh-keygen.ts generates Ed25519 keypairs. cloud-init.ts/snapshot-init.ts inject SSH keys. CLI wired at clawhq deploy update. Remaining work: test coverage for update.ts (zero tests exist) and AC5 error message fix (should name state file path per PO groom notes).
steps: Write tests for src/cloud/provisioning/update.ts covering: successful config/version/skill update, SSH connection failure, timeout, instance not found, shell escaping correctness
Fix AC5 error message at update.ts line ~50 to include ~/.clawhq/cloud/instances.json path alongside 'run clawhq deploy list'
Add integration test: mock SSH provider, verify correct remote commands sent for each mode (config: clawhq build && clawhq restart, version: clawhq update --yes, skill: clawhq skill <args>)
Run npm test to confirm all tests pass
acceptanceCriteria: clawhq deploy update --id <agent-id> --config pushes updated blueprint vars to the remote agent without reprovisioning
clawhq deploy update --id <agent-id> --version pulls latest clawhq, rebuilds Docker image, and restarts the agent on the remote
clawhq deploy update --id <agent-id> --skill install <source> installs a skill on the remote agent
SSH keypair is read from the deploy state file — no manual key management by user
Update fails cleanly if agent-id not found in deploy state — helpful error shown
items.groomingNotes: PO GROOM 2026-03-24 (pass 2): Priority 'must' confirmed. Feature failed once — re-scoped correctly to 'tests + AC5 fix', not rebuild. TECH NOTE: zero tests for shellEscape() is a risk — shell escaping is a security boundary and untested means it could silently pass malformed commands to the remote agent. AC5 error UX (naming the state file path) is a usability requirement, not a nice-to-have — user must know where to look when an agent ID is not found. Sprint-ready as re-scoped. Steps owned by Tech Lead — do not modify.

items.title: Add .doc-standard.yaml schema file for doc structure enforcement
items.intent: The repo must declare its doc standard version and structure so automated checks can validate compliance — undeclared standards are unenforceable.
items.description: Part of a cross-repo documentation standard shared with aishore, GigOS, and other portfolio repos. The .doc-standard.yaml declares: schema version, project metadata (name: ClawHQ, tagline: WordPress for AI agents, category: infrastructure), required files list matching the docs/ structure, cross-repo links to sibling projects (aishore, Easy-Markdown, GigOS), and author info for consistency enforcement across the portfolio. This file enables a future GitHub Action to validate doc structure compliance on PR. Note: ClawHQ has additional docs not in the standard (docs/PRODUCT.md, docs/OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md) — these should be listed in an optional_files section so the validator doesn't flag them as non-standard.
steps: Create .doc-standard.yaml at repo root
Set schema_version to '1.0'
Set project name to 'ClawHQ', tagline to 'WordPress for AI agents', category to 'infrastructure'
List required_files: README.md, docs/ARCHITECTURE.md, docs/QUICKSTART.md, docs/CONFIGURATION.md, docs/PROBLEMS.md, docs/ROADMAP.md, docs/CONTRIBUTING.md, docs/CHANGELOG.md
Add optional_files: docs/PRODUCT.md, docs/OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md (repo-specific docs not in the standard)
Add portfolio section with sibling repos: aishore (complementary), Easy-Markdown (related), GigOS (related)
Add author section: name Simon Plant, location Santa Barbara CA, status 'Building AI infrastructure tools. Open to roles.'
Verify the file is valid YAML
acceptanceCriteria: .doc-standard.yaml exists at repo root and is valid YAML
File declares schema_version, project metadata, required_files, optional_files, portfolio siblings, and author info
required_files matches the docs/ structure created by FEAT-006 through FEAT-012
optional_files lists docs/PRODUCT.md and docs/OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md so they are not flagged as non-standard
Portfolio section lists aishore, Easy-Markdown, and GigOS with correct GitHub URLs
items.groomingNotes: PO GROOM 2026-03-24 (pass 2): Priority 'could' confirmed — no direct user value; this is internal tooling for cross-repo doc validation. FEAT-013 dependency confirmed complete. Sprint only after all must/should items are clear. Self-contained and well-specified — no blockers when bandwidth allows.

items.title: Config surface update for OpenClaw v0.8.6–v0.8.10 environment variables
items.intent: ClawHQ must track OpenClaw's config surface so the config generator, validator, and reference docs stay current. Ten new env vars were added or fixed across v0.8.6–v0.8.10 — they need to be in our schema and docs.
items.description: OpenClaw v0.8.6–v0.8.10 introduced or fixed the following environment variables: WEBSOCKET_EVENT_CALLER_TIMEOUT (configurable WS timeout, was hardcoded 60s), VECTOR_DB=mariadb-vector (new vector DB backend), OPENID_END_SESSION_ENDPOINT (custom OIDC logout), USER_PERMISSIONS_ACCESS_GRANTS_ALLOW_USERS (disable individual sharing), DEFAULT_GROUP_SHARE_PERMISSION, ENABLE_AUDIT_STDOUT (was broken, now works), WEB_SEARCH_DOMAIN_FILTER_LIST (was broken, now works), REPORTING_ENDPOINTS (CSP reporting), OAUTH_UPDATE_NAME_ON_LOGIN, OAUTH_UPDATE_EMAIL_ON_LOGIN. Update: (1) src/config/schema.ts to include new fields, (2) docs/OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md config inventory section, (3) config generator to set sensible defaults where appropriate (especially WEBSOCKET_EVENT_CALLER_TIMEOUT and ENABLE_AUDIT_STDOUT). Also add a minimum version note to OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md — ClawHQ now assumes v0.8.6+ baseline.
acceptanceCriteria: src/config/types.ts includes type definitions for all 10 new environment variables
docs/OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md config inventory lists the new variables with version introduced
Config generator sets WEBSOCKET_EVENT_CALLER_TIMEOUT and ENABLE_AUDIT_STDOUT defaults for new agents
OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md states minimum OpenClaw version as v0.8.6
Existing config validation tests pass with no regression
items.groomingNotes: PO GROOM 2026-03-24 (pass 2): Priority 'should' confirmed. OpenClaw v0.8.6+ is the declared baseline — ClawHQ's config generator must be current or it produces incorrect configs for the baseline version. WEBSOCKET_EVENT_CALLER_TIMEOUT and ENABLE_AUDIT_STDOUT are the highest-value defaults: ENABLE_AUDIT_STDOUT was broken before v0.8.6 and should now default to true for new agents (doctor is the hero feature; audit visibility supports it). Steps are empty — Tech Lead to populate. OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md minimum version note is required for engineering clarity, not optional.

items.title: Doctor checks for OpenClaw v0.8.6+ upgrade issues — migration state, tool access grants, underscore tool methods
items.intent: clawhq doctor must detect upgrade-related issues introduced by OpenClaw v0.8.6–v0.8.10 so users upgrading existing agents don't hit silent failures.
items.description: Three new doctor checks needed: (1) Post-upgrade migration verification — verify database migrations completed (they previously failed silently). (2) Tool access grants — detect tools without explicit access grants and warn that they are admin-only on v0.8.7+ (related to BUG-114 but doctor check is independently useful for existing agents). (3) Underscore tool methods — detect tools with underscore-prefixed public methods that are no longer exposed to the LLM on v0.8.10+. CRITICAL PREREQUISITE: No OpenClaw version detection mechanism exists anywhere in the codebase. Version-gating requires implementing a version query first (options: docker exec to read package.json, parse image tag from docker-compose, or add Gateway RPC version endpoint). Without this, checks must either run unconditionally or this item is blocked.
steps: Implement OpenClaw version detection — determine running version via docker exec, image tag parsing, or Gateway RPC (no mechanism currently exists)
Add version detection to doctor infrastructure (src/operate/doctor/) so checks can query it
Add check: database migration verification (run unconditionally — safe on all versions)
Add check: tool access grants missing (version-gate to v0.8.7+ if version detection works, otherwise run unconditionally with note in output)
Add check: underscore tool methods (version-gate to v0.8.10+ if version detection works, otherwise run unconditionally with note in output)
Coordinate with BUG-114 AC3 — reuse the same tool access grant check logic, don't duplicate
Add tests for all 3 checks and version-gating logic
Run npm test to confirm all tests pass
acceptanceCriteria: clawhq doctor checks that database migrations completed successfully
clawhq doctor warns if any tool server connections lack explicit access grants
clawhq doctor warns if any installed tools have underscore-prefixed methods that were previously LLM-visible
Checks are version-gated when OpenClaw version is detectable; run unconditionally with advisory note when version is unknown
Existing doctor tests pass with no regression
items.groomingNotes: PO GROOM 2026-03-24 (pass 2): Priority 'should' confirmed — doctor is the hero feature. BLOCKING PREREQUISITE: OpenClaw version detection does not exist anywhere in the codebase. If version detection cannot be implemented, checks must run unconditionally (with advisory note in output) — this is the correct fallback per AC4. Do NOT block the entire item on version detection; run unconditionally if needed. COORDINATION REQUIRED: AC2 (tool access grant check) must reuse BUG-114's check logic — do not duplicate. Sprint BUG-114 first, then this. Steps populated by Tech Lead — do not modify.

items.title: End-to-end smoke test — run full user journey against real Docker on local machine
items.intent: 946 unit tests pass but nothing proves the software works. An e2e smoke test must run the actual user journey (install → init → build → up → doctor → down → destroy) against real Docker containers and real filesystem, catching every integration gap the unit tests hide.
items.description: Every external system interaction in ClawHQ is mocked in tests. Docker builds, container lifecycle, filesystem scaffolding, health checks, and config generation are tested in isolation but never together. The documented user journey has never been executed end-to-end. This item creates a smoke test harness that: (1) compiles the binary, (2) runs clawhq install against real filesystem, (3) runs clawhq init with a pre-canned test blueprint (non-interactive), (4) runs clawhq build against real Docker, (5) runs clawhq up and verifies container is running, (6) runs clawhq doctor and checks all 17 checks pass, (7) runs clawhq down, (8) runs clawhq destroy. Each step captures pass/fail and stdout/stderr. Failures are the backlog — this test IS the top-down validation.
steps: Create test/e2e/ directory for end-to-end tests
Create a minimal test blueprint YAML that requires no external API keys (local Ollama or mock LLM)
Create a non-interactive init path or pre-canned config fixture that bypasses the wizard
Write smoke-test.sh or smoke-test.test.ts that executes each CLI command in sequence
Each step: capture exit code, stdout, stderr; assert expected outcomes (files created, container running, etc.)
Add npm run test:e2e script to package.json (separate from unit tests — requires Docker)
Run the smoke test, document every failure, add each as a backlog item
Integrate into CI as an optional job (requires Docker-in-Docker or privileged runner)
acceptanceCriteria: npm run test:e2e runs the full user journey: install → init → build → up → doctor → down → destroy
Test uses real Docker (not mocked) and real filesystem (temp directory, cleaned up after)
Test uses a minimal blueprint that requires no external API keys
Each step reports pass/fail with captured output for debugging
Test is separate from unit tests (does not run on npm test) because it requires Docker
Every failure found during initial run is filed as a backlog item
items.groomingNotes: SCAN 2026-03-24: This is the most important item in the backlog. Every other item is fixing a piece — this item proves whether the pieces work together. Depends on BUG-115 (clawhq build stub) because the build step is mandatory in the journey. Design decision: shell script vs vitest e2e test? Shell script is simpler and more portable; vitest gives better assertions and CI integration.

items.title: Integration tests for cloud provider APIs — replace fetch mocks with contract tests
items.intent: Cloud provider API integrations (DigitalOcean, Hetzner, GCP, AWS) are tested entirely against mocked fetch responses. If a provider changes their API response format, ClawHQ will break silently. Contract tests must verify our code handles real API shapes.
items.description: 10 test files mock globalThis.fetch to simulate cloud provider API responses. The mocks return hardcoded JSON that may not match the real API. Four providers are implemented: DigitalOcean (src/cloud/provisioning/providers/digitalocean.ts), Hetzner (hetzner.ts), GCP (gcp.ts), AWS (aws.ts). Each has a test file with fetch mocked. No test ever hits a real API or validates against a real response schema. This item adds contract tests: (1) record real API responses (sanitized) as fixtures, (2) validate our parsing logic against those fixtures, (3) optionally run live API tests when credentials are available (CI secret). This does NOT remove unit test mocks — it adds a second layer of validation.
steps: Record real API responses from each provider (sanitized — no real IPs, keys, or account IDs)
Create fixture files: test/fixtures/providers/{digitalocean,hetzner,gcp,aws}/*.json
Write contract tests that parse real API response shapes through our provider code
Add optional live API test mode: if CLAWHQ_TEST_DO_TOKEN is set, run against real DigitalOcean API (create+destroy a $4 droplet)
Add npm run test:contract script
Document how to run live tests safely in CONTRIBUTING.md
acceptanceCriteria: Contract tests validate provider code against real API response shapes (recorded fixtures)
Tests catch response format changes that would break parsing (field renames, type changes, new required fields)
Live API tests run when provider credentials are available, skip gracefully when not
Existing unit tests with mocks continue to pass unchanged
items.groomingNotes: SCAN 2026-03-24: Found during deep mock scan. 4 provider files, 4 test files, all with mocked fetch. The mocks were likely written from API docs, not recorded from real responses — drift is probable. Contract tests are the right middle ground between full mocks and expensive live tests.

items.title: Integration tests for prerequisite detection — test against real Docker, Node, Ollama binaries
items.intent: Prerequisite detection (Docker version, Node version, Ollama availability) is tested with mocked child_process.execFile. If the output format of 'docker --version' changes, or if our version parsing regex is wrong, we won't know until a user hits it.
items.description: src/build/installer/prereqs.test.ts mocks the entire node:child_process module. The mock provides helpers like succeedsWith() and fails() to simulate command output. The real execFile() is never called. This means: (1) version parsing regexes are tested against hardcoded strings, not real binary output, (2) error handling for 'command not found' is simulated, not real, (3) Docker socket permissions, PATH issues, and platform differences are invisible. This item adds integration tests that run real binaries when available and skip gracefully when not.
steps: Add integration test file: src/build/installer/prereqs.integration.test.ts
Test: if Docker is available, run the real version check and verify parsing succeeds
Test: if Node is available (it always is in CI), run the real version check
Test: if Ollama is available, run the real availability check
Skip tests gracefully when binaries are not installed (not failures)
Run on CI where Docker is available (GitHub Actions ubuntu-latest has Docker)
acceptanceCriteria: Integration tests run real prerequisite checks against installed binaries
Version parsing succeeds against real Docker and Node output
Tests skip gracefully on machines where Docker or Ollama is not installed
Existing mock-based unit tests remain unchanged
items.groomingNotes: SCAN 2026-03-24: Found during deep mock scan. Lower priority — prereq detection is simple string parsing and unlikely to break. But it's also easy to test for real. Quick win.

items.title: Integration tests for cloud provisioning engine — test real registry, health polling, and state transitions
items.intent: The provisioning engine (engine.ts) is tested with every dependency mocked — credentials, provider adapter, health poller, and registry are all fakes. State transitions (provisioning → active → destroyed) have never been tested against real state persistence.
items.description: src/cloud/provisioning/engine.test.ts mocks 4 modules: credentials.js (returns fake API key), providers/digitalocean.js (returns fake VM creation results), health.js (returns fake health status), registry.js (uses vi.importActual with overrides). The real provisioning engine orchestrates: validate credentials → create firewall → create VM → poll health → register instance → return. None of these steps have been tested in sequence with real state. The registry writes to instances.json — this has never been tested with a real file. Health polling uses real HTTP — this has never been tested against a real (or local test) server. This item creates integration tests that use real filesystem state and a local test HTTP server for health checks, with only the cloud provider API mocked (since we can't create real VMs in CI).
steps: Create integration test: engine.integration.test.ts
Use real filesystem (mkdtempSync) for registry state (instances.json)
Use real health polling against a local HTTP test server (like gateway/client.test.ts does with WebSocketServer)
Mock only the provider adapter (can't create real VMs in CI)
Test full state machine: provision → health check → register → status query → destroy → verify cleanup
Test error paths: provision succeeds but health fails, destroy fails, registry write fails
Run npm test to confirm no regression
acceptanceCriteria: Integration test exercises the full provisioning state machine with real filesystem and real HTTP
Only the cloud provider API is mocked (VM creation/destruction) — everything else is real
Registry state is verified by reading the actual instances.json file, not mocked return values
Health polling is tested against a real local HTTP server, not mocked
All existing engine unit tests continue to pass
items.groomingNotes: SCAN 2026-03-24: Found during deep mock scan. engine.test.ts is the most heavily mocked test file in the codebase (4 modules mocked, 15+ vi.fn() calls). The test verifies that the engine calls the right mocks in the right order — it does not verify that the engine produces correct state. gateway/client.test.ts already demonstrates the pattern of testing against a local server.

items.title: Integration tests for credential probes — test against real API endpoints with test keys
items.intent: Credential health probes (Anthropic, OpenAI, Telegram, etc.) are tested with mocked fetch. If an API changes its auth error response, our probe will misinterpret it. Real probes against real APIs must be tested.
items.description: src/secure/credentials/probes.test.ts mocks globalThis.fetch for all credential probes. The probes check API key validity by making a lightweight API call (e.g., Anthropic /v1/messages with max_tokens:1). The mocks return simulated 200/401/403 responses. This item adds optional live probe tests: when a real API key is available (via env var), run the actual probe against the real API. These tests validate that: (1) our request format is accepted by the real API, (2) the response parsing handles real response shapes, (3) error codes are correctly interpreted.
steps: Add integration test: src/secure/credentials/probes.integration.test.ts
For each probe (Anthropic, OpenAI, Telegram, Ollama): if env var with test key is set, run real probe
Ollama probe can always run if Ollama is installed locally (no API key needed)
Validate: probe returns valid:true for good keys, valid:false for bad keys, correct error messages
Skip gracefully when no test keys are available
Document which env vars to set in CONTRIBUTING.md
acceptanceCriteria: Live probe tests run against real APIs when test credentials are available
Ollama probe runs against local Ollama when available (no key needed)
Tests validate real response parsing, not just mocked shapes
Tests skip gracefully when no credentials are provided — not failures
Existing mock-based unit tests remain unchanged
items.groomingNotes: SCAN 2026-03-24: Found during deep mock scan. Lower priority — probe API calls are simple HTTP and unlikely to break. But Ollama probe can run for free locally, making it a quick win for at least one provider.

items.title: Reduce process.exit() usage in CLI — return exit codes or throw for testability
items.intent: Make CLI command handlers testable and allow proper cleanup. Commands should throw or return exit codes instead of calling process.exit() directly.
items.description: Code review found 107+ direct process.exit() calls in src/cli/index.ts. This prevents testing error paths, blocks defer/cleanup handlers, and makes graceful shutdown impossible. Commands should throw errors or return codes, with a single top-level handler calling process.exit().
steps: Create a CommandError class that carries an exit code
Replace process.exit() calls with throw CommandError in command handlers
Add try/finally blocks around spinner usage to ensure cleanup
Wire top-level parseAsync catch to extract exit codes from CommandError
Add unit tests for error paths in key commands (deploy, destroy, scan)
acceptanceCriteria: Command handlers throw errors or return exit codes instead of calling process.exit()
A single top-level error handler in the CLI entry point calls process.exit()
Spinners are properly stopped in error paths via try/finally
At least 3 command handlers have unit tests covering error paths

items.title: Add manifest version checking on all JSON manifest reads across evolve/cloud
items.intent: Prevent silent data corruption when manifest formats change. Every manifest read must validate the version field and fail clearly on unsupported versions.
items.description: Code review found that multiple modules write manifest files with version: 1 but never validate version on read. If the manifest format changes in a future version, old code will parse new manifests incorrectly — either silently producing wrong data or crashing with confusing errors. Affects skill manifests, export manifests, destruction proofs, and cloud state files.
steps: Identify all manifest read locations across evolve/ and cloud/ modules
Add version validation to each manifest reader (reject if version !== expected)
Add descriptive error messages mentioning the expected vs actual version
Add tests for version mismatch on skill, export, destruction, and cloud manifests
acceptanceCriteria: All manifest read functions validate the version field before parsing
Unsupported manifest versions produce a clear error message
Tests cover version mismatch scenarios for each manifest type
